summary: Marian Ilitch reflects on her career co-founding Little Caesars and solely owning MotorCity Casino Hotel. She emphasizes the importance of balancing marketing vision with financial due diligence, learning continuously from others, relying on common sense, and accepting mistakes as a necessary part of taking business risks.

principles.statement: Learn from anyone who does something better than you.
principles.rationale: Knowledge of finance and business comes from the discipline of taking the time to learn from those around you, starting from the ground up.
principles.source_quote: Not a day has gone by when I didn't learn something from someone. If they can do it better than me, I can learn something from them.

principles.statement: You must be willing to make mistakes in order to take risks.
principles.rationale: Believing you can avoid mistakes is living in a dream world, and avoiding them entirely prevents you from taking the risks necessary for success.
principles.source_quote: If you think you won't make mistakes, you're in a dream world. And you're never going to be a risk-taker if you don't.

principles.statement: Balance visionary marketing with rigorous due diligence.
principles.rationale: A successful business requires both an eye for new opportunities and someone to ensure the financial reality makes sense.
principles.source_quote: Mike is the marketing person, and he's always had his eye on the opportunities out there. I'm the one who does what we'll call the due diligence. And that's worked extremely well.

mental_models.name: The Visionary / Due Diligence Balance
mental_models.description: Viewing business leadership as a necessary partnership between someone who chases opportunities (marketing/vision) and someone who grounds them in financial reality (due diligence).
mental_models.example: When her husband gave away the first two dinners for free to celebrate their restaurant's opening, Marian stepped in on the third customer to charge $3 so there would actually be money in the cash register.

heuristics: If they can do it better than me, I can learn something from them.
Rely on common sense at the heart of all learning.

signature_quotes.text: I didn't recognize how important it was at the time that I made that decision to stop working out of the house and go work full time in the office. That decision was a pivotal point in my life.
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on the decision to become the full-time accountant for their growing company rather than staying home.

signature_quotes.text: Not a day has gone by when I didn't learn something from someone. If they can do it better than me, I can learn something from them.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing how she built her knowledge of finance and business.

signature_quotes.text: I'm blessed with common sense, and it took me awhile to realize that not everyone has that.
signature_quotes.context: Joking about what lies at the heart of her ability to learn.

signature_quotes.text: I'm an achiever. I think of myself as an achiever first, and as a woman second.
signature_quotes.context: Offering advice to others on accepting challenges and working hard.

signature_quotes.text: If you think you won't make mistakes, you're in a dream world. And you're never going to be a risk-taker if you don't.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the relationship between making mistakes and taking risks.

signature_quotes.text: I told her, 'Hey, I buy a dozen roses and put them on my desk every week. That's when I smell the roses.'
signature_quotes.context: Responding to someone asking when she was going to retire and 'stop and smell the roses.'

anti_patterns.description: Believing you can avoid making mistakes in business.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It puts you in a 'dream world' and prevents you from taking the risks necessary to grow and succeed.
